1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-$\geq 2$ extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local $\Rightarrow$ Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ to a regular morphism $J \to A$,
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, a smooth variety $X$ over $\bar k$ (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over $\bar k$ that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety $A$ over $\bar k$ (in particular $A$ is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in $\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k$). A rational map
40	$f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism $\varphi_U \colon U \to A$ defined on a dense open subset
42	$U \subseteq X$, two such pairs $(U, \varphi_U)$ and $(U', \varphi_{U'})$ being equivalent
43	if $\varphi_U$ and $\varphi_{U'}$ agree on $U \cap U'$.
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
49	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
50	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
51	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
52	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
53	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
54	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
55	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map $\alpha\colon V--\to A$ from a nonsingular
56	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of $V$."
57	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
58	
59	  Let $X$ be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, let $A$ be
60	  an abelian variety over $\bar k$, and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow A$ be a rational
61	  map. Then $f$ is defined on the whole of $X$: there is a unique regular morphism
62	  $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$ whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
63	  set of definition $U \subseteq X$ of $f$ agrees with $\varphi_U$.
64	\end{theorem}
65	
66	The uniqueness clause is immediate from $X$ being reduced and separated: two regular
67	morphisms $X \to A$ that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
68	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-$\geq 2$
69	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
70	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
71	
72	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
73	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
74	\begin{proof}
75	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
76	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
77	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
78	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
79	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
80	  pure-codimension-$1$ structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
81	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
82	
83	  Concretely: let $U \subseteq X$ be a maximal open set of definition of $f$, and let
84	  $Z := X \setminus U$ be its complement.
85	  \begin{enumerate}
86	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
87	      to codim $\geq 2$), applied with the normal variety $X$ and the complete variety
88	      $A$, we have $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
89	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-$1$
90	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety $A$), the closed subset
91	      $Z$ is either empty or of pure codimension $1$ in $X$.
92	    \item Both conditions together force $Z = \emptyset$: a non-empty pure-codimension-$1$
93	      closed subset has codimension exactly $1$, contradicting $\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2$.
94	  \end{enumerate}
95	  Therefore $U = X$ and $f$ admits a regular representative on all of $X$, which is the
96	  required regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X \to A$. Uniqueness is the standard
97	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
98	\end{proof}
99	
100	\begin{remark}
101	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
102	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
103	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
104	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-$1$ step
105	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
106	  $\mathcal O_{X, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ --- works because $X$ is normal
107	  and $A$ is complete) and a codim-$\geq 2$ step (depth-$\geq 2$ at codim-$\geq 2$ points,
108	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
109	  depth-$\geq 2$ input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
110	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
111	  for a regular local ring $(\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)$ of Krull dimension $d$, the
112	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to $M = R$
113	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
114	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d$, so in particular
115	  $\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2$ whenever $\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2$.
116	  Concretely, when $X$ is a smooth surface (e.g.\ $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ or
117	  $C \times C$ for a smooth projective curve $C$), every closed point $x$ is regular of
118	  dimension $2$, so $\mathcal O_{X, x}$ is Cohen--Macaulay of depth $2$; sections of
119	  $\mathcal O_X$ on $U = X \setminus \{x\}$ extend uniquely across $x$, and the same is
120	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of $\mathcal O_A$ along $f$.
121	
122	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
123	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-$\geq 2$ half. The dependency
124	  $\uses{thm:auslander_buchsbaum, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}$ is recorded on
125	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
126	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
127	\end{remark}
128	
129	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
130	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
131	
132	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
133	\begin{itemize}
134	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-$\geq 2$ extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
135	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-$\geq 2$ extension
136	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
137	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
138	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
139	    $\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A$ and
140	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
141	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
142	    on $A$ (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of $X$ (so divisors and
143	    DVRs at codim-$1$ points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
144	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
145	    has a characteristic restriction.
146	\end{itemize}
147	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
148	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
149	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
150	
151	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
152	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
153	
154	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
155	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian $J = \Pic^0_{C/k}$. Milne's proof of
156	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
157	morphism $\psi \colon J \to A$ factoring a given pointed regular morphism
158	$\varphi \colon C \to A$ through the canonical $f_P \colon C \to J$ as follows. The
159	symmetric power $C^{(g)}$ admits a regular morphism $\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A$
160	defined by
161	\[
162	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
163	\]
164	and a birational morphism $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J$ (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
165	seeks a morphism $J \to A$ making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
166	$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ followed by descent to $J$; the rational map
167	$C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
168	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because $C^{(g)}$ is
169	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and $A$ is an abelian variety. Without
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
171	shortcut ($J \cong J^\vee$) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
172	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
173	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
174	
175	\section{Lean encoding}
176	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
177	
178	The Lean target file is the new
179	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}. The single declaration
180	this chapter targets is the theorem
181	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}, with signature (in informal
182	notation):
183	\[
184	  \texttt{X : Scheme} \;\to\; \texttt{[Smooth X over $\bar k$]} \;\to\;
185	  \texttt{A : AbelianVariety $\bar k$} \;\to\;
186	  (f : \texttt{X.RationalMap A}) \;\to\;
187	  \exists ! \, \widetilde f \colon X \to A, \;\; \widetilde f \;\text{extends}\; f.
188	\]
189	The proof body imports \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s exports and
190	\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}'s exports from
191	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean} and
192	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} respectively, and combines them
193	in the three-step pattern above (codim $\geq 2$ from A.4.a + pure-codim $1$ from A.4.a
194	$\Rightarrow$ empty indeterminacy locus).
195	
196	The Mathlib namespace target is
197	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap} (already housed in
198	\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap} at the project's pinned commit;
199	see \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, where the same namespace hosts the
200	order-at-a-prime-divisor map). The extension is therefore a method on the existing
201	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure rather than a free-standing predicate, allowing
202	downstream consumers in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP} to call it as
203	\texttt{f.extend\_to\_av}.
204	
205	No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a two-line combination of A.4.a and
206	A.4.b. The chapter is therefore inexpensive on the prover side (the LOC budget is dominated
207	by A.4.a and A.4.b themselves); its purpose is to record the precise statement and the
208	exact dependency wiring so that A.4.d can consume a single named lemma.
209	
210	\section{Out of scope}
211	\label{sec:thm32_out_of_scope}
212	
213	This chapter packages \emph{only} the theorem
214	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne~3.2) and the immediate proof combining its
215	two inputs. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of scope here:
216	\begin{itemize}
217	  \item \textbf{The codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem (Milne Theorem~3.1) itself.}
218	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
219	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; this chapter only consumes it.
220	  \item \textbf{The pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy lemma (Milne Lemma~3.3) itself.}
221	    Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} as
222	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; this chapter only consumes it.
223	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and its Cohen--Macaulay corollary
224	    (Milne~$\equiv$ Stacks 090V / 00OD).} Proved in
225	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and
226	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this chapter only consumes them (indirectly,
227	    inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see \cref{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}).
228	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
229	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the special cases ``rational map
230	    from a product of nonsingular varieties to an AV vanishing on the axes is constant'',
231	    ``rational map between AVs = homomorphism + translation'', ``birationally equivalent
232	    AVs are isomorphic'', ``every $\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A$ is constant'',
233	    ``every rational map from a unirational variety to an AV is constant'') is downstream
234	    of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not needed by A.4 in the project's
235	    current routing: the genus-$0$ base case
236	    \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route} closes
237	    via the proven Rigidity Lemma + $\mathbb G_m$-scaling shortcut without invoking any of
238	    these. They are recorded as off-path in
239	    \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}.
240	  \item \textbf{The Albanese universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ (Milne Proposition~6.1).}
241	    Consumed by A.4.d, proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}; this chapter only
242	    provides the technical extension lemma that A.4.d invokes when promoting
243	    $C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A$ to $C^{(g)} \to A$.
244	  \item \textbf{Generalisations beyond an algebraically closed base field.} Milne's
245	    Theorem~3.2 is stated for varieties over an algebraically closed field; the
246	    project's positive-genus arm runs over $\bar k$ throughout (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}).
247	    Descent of the extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would
248	    require additional Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
249	\end{itemize}

9	# Relative spectrum of a quasi-coherent sheaf of algebras (A.1.a)
10	
11	This file is the **A.1.a** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the relative-spectrum
13	functor `Spec_X(𝒜) : QcohAlg(X)^op ⥤ Sch/X` used by the relative Picard functor on a
14	product `C ×_k T`.
15

83	/-! ## §1. Quasi-coherent sheaves of `O_X`-algebras
84	
85	For a scheme `X`, a quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras is a sheaf of
86	`O_X`-algebras whose underlying `O_X`-module is quasi-coherent. Stacks tag
87	01LL packages the notion as a sheaf $\mathcal{A}$ on $X$ taking values in
88	commutative rings together with a unit map from the structure sheaf
89	$\mathcal{O}_X$; equivalently, an object of the under-category
90	$\mathcal{O}_X / \operatorname{Sh}(X, \mathbf{CommRing})$.
91	
92	iter-174 (Lane G) instantiates this as **Encoding I** (sheafified
93	`Under`-object form): a pair of a sheaf of commutative rings and a unit
94	morphism from the structure sheaf. The quasi-coherence overlay
95	(`SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` after threading the
96	`HasSheafCompose`/`HasWeakSheafify`/`WEqualsLocallyBijective` instances) is
97	iter-175+ work — it is the *refinement* of "sheaf of `O_X`-algebras" to
98	"quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras" and requires the sheafified
99	tensor-product infrastructure on `SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf`, which is
100	missing from Mathlib at the pinned commit (`b80f227`; cf.
101	`analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md`). The Encoding I shape is upgradable to
102	that refinement by adding a `Prop`-valued field once the predicate is in
103	scope.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras` (Stacks 01LL,
106	situation-relative-spec). -/
107	
108	/-- A **quasi-coherent sheaf of `O_X`-algebras** (Encoding I — Lane G iter-174).
109	
110	A pair of
111	- `sheaf` : a sheaf of commutative rings on the underlying topological space of
112	  `X`, and
113	- `unit` : an `O_X`-algebra unit, i.e. a morphism of sheaves of commutative
114	  rings `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf` from the structure sheaf to the carrier.
115	
116	This is the *sheafified under-object* form of "commutative `O_X`-algebra": the
117	direct upgrade of Mathlib's `Under (R : CommRingCat)` idiom (cf.
118	`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/Ring/Under/Basic.lean`,
119	*"`Under R` is (equivalent to) the category of commutative `R`-algebras"*) to
120	the relative setting where `R` is replaced by the sheaf of rings `X.sheaf`.
121	
122	iter-175+ will add a third field `isQcoh : Prop` carrying the
123	quasi-coherence predicate (the underlying `O_X`-module is quasi-coherent),
124	once the sheafified-tensor / `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` infrastructure
125	is available in Mathlib; see `analogies/qcohalgebra-structure.md` Decision 1
126	for the upstream-gap analysis. The Encoding I shape is upgradable to the
127	fully-overlayed Encoding II (monoid-in-`SheafOfModules`) without renaming —
128	when monoidal `SheafOfModules` lands upstream, both fields transport via the
129	equivalence `Mon_(SheafOfModules X.ringCatSheaf) ≃ Under X.sheaf in
130	Sheaf CommRingCat`. -/
131	structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where
132	  /-- The underlying sheaf of commutative rings on `X`. -/
133	  sheaf : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace
134	  /-- The `O_X`-algebra unit `X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf` exhibiting `sheaf` as a sheaf
135	  of `O_X`-algebras. -/
136	  unit : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf

235

238	quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra and

242	lemma-spec-base-change). -/

247	exists a quasi-coherent `O_T`-algebra `𝒜' = g^* 𝒜` and a canonical isomorphism
248	of `T`-schemes
249	`T ×_X Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec_T(g^* 𝒜)`.

251	The pullback `g^* 𝒜 : T.QcohAlgebra` is bound by the existential; iter-174+

258	an `O_S`-algebra map `(f')^*(g^* 𝒜) = f^* 𝒜 → O_S`, i.e. an element of the
259	functor for `(T, g^* 𝒜)`. -/

282	iter-174+: the body is concrete via `Over.mk (RelativeSpec.structureMorphism 𝒜)`

62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,

100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-173+: the body extracts the DVR `O_{X,η}` (from `(*)` regularity), reads off the
138	`addVal` (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal : R → ℕ∞`), extends it to the fraction field
139	`K(X)`, and produces an `ℤ`-valued valuation on `K(X)^×`. -/
140	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
141	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
142	  sorry
143	
144	end Scheme.RationalMap
145	
146	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
147	
148	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

150	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
151	
152	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
153	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
154	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
155	
156	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
157	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
158	
159	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
160	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
161	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
162	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
163	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
164	
165	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
166	
167	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
168	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
169	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
170	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
171	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
172	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
173	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
174	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
175	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
176	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
177	hypothesised regime. -/
178	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
179	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
180	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
181	
182	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
183	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
184	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
185	multiplicity one. -/
186	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
187	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
188	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
189	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
190	
191	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
192	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
193	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
194	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
195	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
196	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
197	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
198	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
199	
200	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
201	
202	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
203	
204	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
205	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
206	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
207	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
208	
209	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
210	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
211	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
212	
213	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
214	
215	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
216	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
217	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
218	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
219	
220	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
221	
222	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
223	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
224	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
225	
226	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
227	
228	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
229	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
230	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
231	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
232	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
233	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
234	
235	@[simp]
236	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
237	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
238	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
239	
240	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
241	
242	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
243	
244	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
245	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
246	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
247	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
248	
249	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
250	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
253	following definition, p. 131).
254	
255	iter-173+: the body invokes Hartshorne 6.1 for the finite-support side condition,
256	then materialises the `Finsupp` whose value at each prime divisor `Y` is
257	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f`. -/
258	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] (f : X.functionField)
259	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
260	  sorry
261	
262	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
263	
264	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
265	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
266	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
267	
268	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
269	
270	iter-173+: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`,
271	`v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0` (`AddValuation.map_add`, etc.), summing over the
272	union of supports. -/
273	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] :
274	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor :=
275	  sorry
276	
277	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
278	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
279	
280	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
281	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
282	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
283	
284	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
285	
286	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
287	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
288	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
289	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
290	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
291	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
292	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
293	iters of `RR.1`. -/
294	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
295	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
296	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
297	    [IsIntegral C.left] (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
298	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 :=
299	  sorry

300	
301	/-! ## §6. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
302	
303	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
304	
305	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
306	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
307	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
308	
309	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
310	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
311	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
312	**divisor class group** of `X`.
313	
314	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
315	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
316	
317	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
318	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
319	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
320	
321	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
322	
323	end AlgebraicGeometry

1	# Analogy: extend a rational map from a smooth projective surface to a proper group scheme without Weil divisors
2

4	cross-domain-inspiration
5

12	## Structural problem (abstracted)
13	Given a morphism defined on a dense open `U ⊆ X` of an integral regular scheme into a
14	target `Y` whose structure morphism is proper, decide where it extends. Two sub-shapes:
15	(1) **codim-1 extension** — extend across every height-1 point (the local ring is a
16	DVR); (2) **emptiness** — fill the remaining codim-≥2 locus, which for a *proper* target
17	is only possible when extra structure (a group law on `Y`) is present. The blocker is
18	that the classical emptiness proof (Milne Lemma 3.3) is phrased with Weil divisors, and
19	Mathlib has no Weil/Cartier divisor API at scheme generality.
20	
21	## Failed approaches (from directive context)
22	- Hand-building Weil-divisor theory (`div(f)`, prime divisors) on `ℙ¹×ℙ¹`: Mathlib has
23	  no usable scheme-level divisor API; building one is out of scope.
24	
25	## Key finding up front
26	Mathlib **already ships the entire codim-1 / "Theorem 3.1" half** as a *different
27	sub-area of algebraic geometry the project has not been using*: the
28	`AlgebraicGeometry.RationalMap` + `ValuativeCriterion` + `SpreadingOut` stack. The
29	"defined at every codim-1 point ⟺ complement has codim ≥ 2" statement is **pure
30	properness, no group, no divisors**, and is fully portable today. The emptiness step
31	(Lemma 3.3) is genuinely *not* a valuative side-step — the valuative criterion is
32	structurally blind to codim-≥2 points (a DVR is *exactly* a codim-1 local ring; there is
33	no "codim-2 valuative criterion"). For a general proper target the codim-2 locus cannot
34	be filled (`ℙ²⇢ℙ¹` at `[0:0:1]`), so the group law is essential there.
35	
36	## Analogues found
37	
38	### Analogue 1: `AlgebraicGeometry.ValuativeCriterion.Existence` + `Scheme.PartialMap.ofFromSpecStalk`  (Theorem 3.1, codim ≥ 2)
39	- **Domain**: algebraic geometry — but the *valuative-criterion / spreading-out*
40	  sub-shelf, distinct from the divisor machinery the project has been chasing.
41	- **Same structural problem there**: "a morphism `Spec K → X` over a proper `f : X ⟶ Y`,
42	  given a dominating `Spec R → Y` from a valuation ring `R = Frac⁻¹(K)`, lifts to
43	  `Spec R → X`." That is *exactly* "extend over the DVR `O_{V,Z}` at a height-1 point."
44	- **Technique / exact API**:
45	  - `ValuativeCommSq {X Y} (f : X ⟶ Y)` — `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/ValuativeCriterion.lean:53`.
46	    Bundles `R` (a `ValuationRing`), `K = Frac R`, `i₁ : Spec K ⟶ X`, `i₂ : Spec R ⟶ Y`,
47	    and the commuting square.
48	  - `ValuativeCriterion.Existence : MorphismProperty Scheme` (`ValuativeCriterion.lean:78`)
49	    `:= fun f ↦ ∀ S : ValuativeCommSq f, S.commSq.HasLift`. This is the per-DVR lift
50	    extractor the directive asked for.
51	  - `IsProper.eq_valuativeCriterion` (`ValuativeCriterion.lean:328`):
52	    `@IsProper = ValuativeCriterion ⊓ QuasiCompact ⊓ QuasiSeparated ⊓ LocallyOfFiniteType`.
53	    `rw` of this on `[IsProper A.hom]` yields `ValuativeCriterion A.hom`, hence
54	    `ValuativeCriterion.Existence A.hom`. (Forward direction is free; the project usually
55	    cites the *reverse* `IsProper.of_valuativeCriterion` at line 339.)
56	  - DVR at the codim-1 point, **no global Dedekind hypothesis**:
57	    `IsDiscreteValuationRing.tfae_of_isNoetherianRing_of_isLocalRing_of_isDomain`
58	    (`Mathlib/RingTheory/DiscreteValuationRing/TFAE.lean:168`) makes a *local* Noetherian
59	    domain a DVR from `finrank_cotangentSpace ≤ 1` (`Mathlib/RingTheory/Ideal/Cotangent.lean:349`,
60	    used as `tfae_have 6 ↔ 5`) plus `¬ IsField`. So `O_{V,Z}` regular of dim 1 ⟹ DVR
61	    ⟹ `ValuationRing` (`Mathlib/RingTheory/DiscreteValuationRing/Basic.lean:512` /
62	    `of_isDiscreteValuationRing`). NB the *Dedekind* extractor
63	    `IsLocalization.AtPrime.isDiscreteValuationRing_of_dedekind_domain`
64	    (`Mathlib/RingTheory/DedekindDomain/Dvr.lean:131`) is the **wrong generality** — it
65	    needs the *global* ring dim ≤ 1, which fails for a surface; use the TFAE instead.
66	  - `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk x) X.functionField` for integral `X`
67	    (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/FunctionField.lean:151`) and
68	    `IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk x)` (`:166`) supply `K = K(V)`, `R = O_{V,Z}` directly as
69	    `i₁ = α.fromFunctionField` (`RationalMap.lean:145`).
70	  - **Spreading out** the lift back into an open neighbourhood of `Z`:
71	    `Scheme.PartialMap.ofFromSpecStalk` (`Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/RationalMap.lean:179`),
72	    built on `spread_out_of_isGermInjective'` (`SpreadingOut.lean:367`). Needs
73	    `[LocallyOfFiniteType A.hom]` (have it) and `X.IsGermInjectiveAt Z`, which is free:
74	    `IsIntegral X → X.IsGermInjective` (`SpreadingOut.lean:158`).
75	  - **Gluing** the per-point spreadings into one maximal partial map: `RationalMap.domain`
76	    (`RationalMap.lean:485`, `sSup` over representatives) and `RationalMap.toPartialMap`
77	    (`RationalMap.lean:517`, glued via `openCoverDomain` + `glueMorphisms`; uses `Y`
78	    separated for uniqueness). Each spread-out enlarges `domain`; the union omits only
79	    codim-≥2 points.
80	- **Mapping to project**: set `f := A.hom : A ⟶ Spec k̄` (`[IsProper]`,
81	  `[LocallyOfFiniteType]`). At a height-1 point `Z` of `V` (regular), build a
82	  `ValuativeCommSq A.hom` with `R := V.presheaf.stalk Z`, `K := V.functionField`,
83	  `i₁ := α.fromFunctionField`, `i₂ := Spec.map …` to `Spec k̄` (square commutes by
84	  `Spec k̄`-uniqueness). `Existence` gives the lift `Spec O_{V,Z} ⟶ A`; `ofFromSpecStalk`
85	  spreads it to a neighbourhood, so `Z ∈ α.domain`. Hence `V ∖ α.domain` has codim ≥ 2.
86	- **Porting cost**: medium. The decls all exist; the work is the per-`Z` `ValuativeCommSq`
87	  construction, the DVR instance for `O_{V,Z}` (regular dim-1 via TFAE), and the
88	  scheme-level "no codim-1 point in the complement ⟹ codim ≥ 2" bookkeeping (Mathlib has
89	  no `Scheme` codimension API, so this is the one hand-rolled piece).
90	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.
91	
92	### Analogue 2: the ruling / genus-0-rigidity reduction for the surface `ℙ¹×ℙ¹` (avoids Lemma 3.3 entirely)
93	- **Domain**: algebraic geometry — reuses the project's *own* proven rigidity, a distant
94	  in-repo tool, in place of divisor theory.
95	- **Same structural problem there**: "a rational map out of a product of curves is
96	  constant along each ruling, hence constant, hence extends." Replaces "fill the
97	  codim-2 locus" with "the map was constant all along."
98	- **Technique**: for general `t`, `α|_{ℙ¹×{t}} : ℙ¹ ⇢ A` is a rational map from a smooth
99	  *curve*; the **curve** valuative criterion (Analogue 1 with `dim = 1`, every closed
100	  point's local ring a DVR) extends it to a genuine morphism `ℙ¹ → A`, which is
101	  **constant** by the project's `rigidity_over_kbar` (morphism `ℙ¹_{k̄} → A_{k̄}` constant,
102	  `AlgebraicJacobian/RigidityKbar.lean:75`). So `α(s,t) = c(t)` independent of `s` on a
103	  dense open; symmetric in `s` ⟹ `α` is constant on a dense open ⟹ extends as the
104	  constant morphism (a constant `PartialMap` on a dense open is `equiv` to the constant
105	  morphism on all of `V`).
106	- **Mapping to project**: this is the cheapest path **iff** the obligation is genuinely
107	  the surface `ℙ¹×ℙ¹` (as the directive states) and `rigidity_over_kbar` (or at least
108	  "morphism `ℙ¹→A` constant") is available **upstream** of Theorem 3.2. ⚠ Circularity
109	  check required: the blueprint (`AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex:43`) currently derives
110	  "`ℙ¹→A` constant" *through* Thm 3.2 + Cor 1.2/1.5. If the only available proof of
111	  "`ℙ¹→A` constant" routes through Thm 3.2, this reduction is circular and must instead
112	  use the differential/`H⁰(ℙ¹,Ω)=0` argument the blueprint mentions
113	  (`AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex:26`) or `rigidity_lemma` (Mumford Form I, proven
114	  axiom-clean iter-162) directly. Also: only works for ruled / product-of-curve sources,
115	  not a general nonsingular `V`.
116	- **Porting cost**: low–medium *if* the upstream constancy is available; the curve
117	  valuative step is a special case of Analogue 1 and the genericity ("general fiber meets
118	  the dense domain") is elementary.
119	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND (conditional on the circularity check).
120	
121	### Analogue 3: `RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.ord` — the divisor-free *local* multiplicity (only substitute for Lemma 3.3's divisor content)
122	- **Domain**: commutative algebra.
123	- **Same structural problem there**: "assign a codim-1 multiplicity without a global
124	  divisor group." `ord R x := Module.length R (R ⧸ span{x})`
125	  (`Mathlib/RingTheory/OrderOfVanishing/Basic.lean:35`), with `ord_mul`, `ord_pow`,
126	  `ord_le_ord_of_dvd`, etc. At a DVR this is the valuation; it is the length-theoretic
127	  order of vanishing along a height-1 prime.
128	- **Technique**: replaces `div(f)` *locally* (one prime at a time) by a length, dodging a
129	  global Weil-divisor group.
130	- **Mapping to project**: if one insisted on Milne's actual Lemma 3.3 difference-map
131	  argument, `ord` is the only Mathlib tool for "the order of `f` along `Z`." But Lemma 3.3
132	  also needs **codim-1 = locally principal** = regular local rings are UFDs
133	  (Auslander–Buchsbaum), which **Mathlib does not have** (no `IsRegularLocalRing →
134	  UniqueFactorizationMonoid`; confirmed absent). So `ord` alone is insufficient.
135	- **Porting cost**: high — would still require an Auslander–Buchsbaum gap-fill plus a
136	  divisor-of-poles construction.
137	- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE.
138	
139	## Direct answer to the open question
140	**No** — there is no pointwise-valuative side-step that makes the indeterminacy locus
141	*empty*. The valuative criterion at height-1 primes gives precisely "defined at every
142	codim-1 point" = complement codim ≥ 2 (Theorem 3.1), for free from properness and with
143	**no divisors and no group** (Analogue 1). It cannot reach codim 0: a DVR is a codim-1
144	object, so the criterion never sees codim-≥2 points, and for a general proper target
145	those points genuinely obstruct extension (`ℙ²⇢ℙ¹`). The emptiness (Lemma 3.3) requires
146	the group law; Milne's proof of it needs codim-1-is-principal (Auslander–Buchsbaum),
147	absent from Mathlib. The cheap escape is to *avoid Lemma 3.3 altogether* via the ruling /
148	genus-0-rigidity reduction (Analogue 2) for the `ℙ¹×ℙ¹` case the directive actually needs.
149	
150	## Top suggestion
151	Adopt the `RationalMap` + `ValuativeCriterion` + `SpreadingOut` substrate immediately for
152	the codim-1 half — it is shipped Mathlib the project is not yet importing (only two
153	incidental `Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι` uses exist in
154	`AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:700` / `Rigidity.lean:113`). Concretely: phrase
155	`rationalMap_to_av_extends` against `Scheme.RationalMap` / `RationalMap.domain`, get
156	codim-≥2 from `ValuativeCriterion.Existence (A.hom)` (via `IsProper.eq_valuativeCriterion`)
157	+ `ofFromSpecStalk`, with `O_{V,Z}` made a DVR through
158	`tfae_of_isNoetherianRing_of_isLocalRing_of_isDomain` + `finrank_cotangentSpace_le_one_iff`.
159	For the final emptiness, try Analogue 2 (rulings + `rigidity_over_kbar`) **after** clearing
160	the circularity check, rather than building divisors or Auslander–Buchsbaum. First file to
161	touch: a new `AlgebraicJacobian/RationalMapExtend.lean` importing
162	`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.RationalMap` and `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ValuativeCriterion`.
163

8	/-!
9	# Line-bundle pullback on a relative curve (A.1.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.1.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the line-bundle
13	pullback functor `π_T^* : Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)` along the projection
14	`π_T : C ×_S T → T` of a relative curve, and the set-valued relative Picard
15	presheaf
16	
17	```
18	Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) := Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)
19	```
20	
21	as a functor on `(Sch/S)^op`.
22	
23	## Status (iter-174 Lane E file-skeleton)
24	
25	This file is the **iter-174 Lane E** file-skeleton: each of the five pinned
26	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
27	blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/Picard_LineBundlePullback.tex`) with a
28	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-175+ work after the sibling chapters
29	`Picard_RelativeSpec.lean` (A.1.a) settles its `QcohAlgebra` body, and after
30	`Picard_RelPicFunctor.lean` (A.1.c) lands the étale-sheafification overlay.
31	
32	The 5 pinned declarations are:
33	
34	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct` (def, ~5 LOC) — the type of
35	   line bundles on the product `C ×_S T`.
36	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection`
37	   (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the pullback map `Pic(T) → Pic(C ×_S T)`.
38	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
39	   — composition of pullbacks `g_C^* ∘ π_T^* = π_{T'}^* ∘ g^*` (Stacks 01HG).
40	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup` (def, ~8 LOC)
41	   — well-definedness of the quotient `Pic(C ×_S T) / π_T^* Pic(T)`, encoded as
42	   the substantive `Setoid` on `OnProduct πC πT` that defines the equivalence
43	   relation `L ~ L' ↔ L ⊗ (L')⁻¹ ∈ π_T^* Pic(T)`.
44	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial`
45	   (noncomputable def, ~10 LOC) — for `g : T' ⟶ T` over `S`, the induced map
46	   `Pic^♯_{C/S}(T) → Pic^♯_{C/S}(T')` factoring `g_C^*` through the quotient.
47	
48	## Note on type expressivity
49	
50	Because Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `Module.Invertible`/`IsInvertible` predicate
51	on `Scheme.Modules` (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for `R : CommRing`),
52	the type `OnProduct` packaging *line bundles* (= invertible sheaves of
53	`O`-modules) on the product cannot yet be carved out of `Scheme.Modules` as a
54	subtype. Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof"
55	we encode `OnProduct` by a typed `sorry` at the type level: the iter-175+ body
56	will instantiate it as a structure pairing a `(pullback πC πT).Modules` carrier
57	with an `IsInvertible` witness once that predicate is in Mathlib (or proven
58	internally as the project-side definition).
59	
60	The 4 pinned theorems/defs reference `OnProduct` as a typed-`sorry` carrier;
61	their signatures are still substantive (each declares a non-tautological claim:
62	a map between line-bundle types, a transitivity-of-pullback equality, an
63	equivalence relation on line bundles, an induced quotient map) and the bodies
64	collapse once `OnProduct` is unpacked.
65	
66	## References
67	
68	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_LineBundlePullback.tex` (444 LOC,
69	5 pins). Source: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
70	Definitions `df:aPf` (absolute Picard functor) and `df:Pfs` (relative Picard
71	functor). Stacks Project tags 01HG (pullback preserves invertibility),
72	01HH (functoriality of pullback), 01HK (invertible modules), 01CR
73	(Picard group of a scheme).
74	-/

96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.

/-

Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
Authors: Christian Merten
-/
import Mathlib

/-!
# Milne Theorem 3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
variety extends (A.4.c)

This file is the **A.4.c** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the single
theorem of Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §I.3, Theorem 3.2:
**a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends,
uniquely, to a regular morphism**. This is the input that the Albanese
universal-property build (A.4.d, `Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`) consumes when
promoting the symmetric-product rational map
`C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism `J → A`.

Milne's proof is two lines: combine Theorem 3.1 (the codim-$\geq 2$ extension
result for rational maps from a normal variety to a complete variety —
project-side `thm:codim_one_extension` of
`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`, A.4.a) with Lemma 3.3 (the
pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy structure for rational maps into a group variety
— project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` of the same chapter). The
combination forces the indeterminacy locus of `f` to be simultaneously of
codimension `≥ 2` and pure codimension `1`, hence empty, so `f` is everywhere
defined.

## Status (iter-175 Lane J file-skeleton)

This file is the **iter-175 Lane J** file-skeleton: the single pinned
declaration carries the intended substantive type signature (matching the
blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in
`chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50) with a `sorry`
body. The body is iter-176+ work, gated on the sibling chapters
`Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean` (A.4.a) and
`Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean` (A.4.b) landing their substantive
statements.

The 1 pinned declaration is:

1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
   — Milne Theorem 3.2: for `X` a smooth (= nonsingular) variety over an
   algebraically closed field `k̄`, `A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, and
   `f : X ⇢ A` a rational map, there exists a *unique* regular morphism
   `g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.

## Note on type expressivity

The signature is encoded against Mathlib's existing
`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap` (a partial-map equivalence class on
dense opens) and `Scheme.Hom.toRationalMap` (the canonical "regular morphism
yields a rational map" coercion). The substantive claim is the existence
and uniqueness of a `g : X.left ⟶ A.left` with `g.toRationalMap = f`. The
type is non-tautological: the existence is the content of Milne 3.2;
uniqueness is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle
(`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`).

## Abelian-variety conventions

Throughout the project (cf. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`, `Jacobian.lean`),
an **abelian variety over `k̄`** is encoded as an object
`A : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying the four instances:

- `[GrpObj A]` (group-object structure on the over-category),
- `[IsProper A.hom]` (complete),
- `[Smooth A.hom]` (nonsingular),
- `[GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]` (geometrically irreducible).

A **nonsingular variety over `k̄`** (in the convention of
`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1) is an
object `X : Over (Spec (.of k̄))` carrying:

- `[Smooth X.hom]`,
- `[GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]`,
- `[IsSeparated X.hom]`,
- `[LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]`,
- `[IsIntegral X.left]`,
- `[IsReduced X.left]`.

These four-plus-two instances will normally be supplied by `inferInstance` at
the call site.

## References

Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex`
(250 LOC, 1 pin). Source: Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, §I.3, Theorem 3.2,
p. 17 (`references/abelian-varieties.pdf`, PDF page 23).
-/

set_option autoImplicit false

universe u

open CategoryTheory Limits

namespace AlgebraicGeometry

namespace Scheme

namespace RationalMap

/-! ## Milne Theorem 3.2 — rational maps to an abelian variety extend

Let `k̄` be an algebraically closed field. Let `X` be a nonsingular variety
over `k̄` (smooth, geometrically irreducible, separated, locally of finite
type, integral, reduced). Let `A` be an abelian variety over `k̄`
(a group object that is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible). For
every rational map `f : X ⇢ A` there is a *unique* regular morphism
`g : X ⟶ A` whose induced rational map equals `f`.

Milne's argument (`Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` §1.4, Theorem 3.2):
let `U ⊆ X` be a maximal open of definition of `f` and `Z := X ∖ U`.
By the codim-$\geq 2$ extension theorem (Theorem 3.1, project-side
`thm:codim_one_extension`) the closed subset `Z` has codimension `≥ 2`.
By the pure-codim-$1$ indeterminacy lemma for maps into a group variety
(Lemma 3.3, project-side `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy`), `Z` is either
empty or of pure codimension `1`. Both conditions force `Z = ∅`, so `U = X`
and `f` admits a regular representative on all of `X`. Uniqueness is the
standard reduced-and-separated agreement principle (cf.
`AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant`): two regular morphisms `X ⟶ A` that
agree on a dense open of `X` agree everywhere.

Blueprint reference: `thm:rational_map_to_av_extends` (Milne, *Abelian
Varieties*, Theorem 3.2, §I.3, p. 17). The pin in the chapter is at
`Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension.tex` line 50.

iter-176+ proof skeleton:
1. Pick a `PartialMap` representative `φ : X.PartialMap A` of `f` (using
   `Scheme.RationalMap.toPartialMap`, which exists for `X` reduced and `A`
   separated; both hold from the abelian-variety + variety instances).
2. Show `φ.domain = ⊤` by combining `thm:codim_one_extension` (codim `≥ 2`
   complement) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (complement empty or
   pure codim `1`).
3. Read off `g := φ.hom` (the morphism on the full domain) and verify
   `g.toRationalMap = f`.
4. Uniqueness: two `g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ A` with `g₁.toRationalMap = g₂.toRationalMap`
   agree on a dense open of `X`, so by
   `AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isDominant` and reducedness of `X` they agree
   everywhere. -/

/-- **Milne Theorem 3.2 — a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
abelian variety extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism.**

For `X` a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, and
`A` an abelian variety over `k̄`, every rational map `f : X ⇢ A` has a
*unique* regular extension `g : X ⟶ A` (i.e. `g.toRationalMap = f`).

This is the input the Albanese universal-property build
(`Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean`, A.4.d) consumes when promoting the
symmetric-product rational map `C^{(g)} ⇢ A` to a regular morphism
`J → A`.

iter-176+: body combines `thm:codim_one_extension` (A.4.a; codim-$\geq 2$
extension to a normal variety target — here the abelian variety, which is
complete) with `lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy` (A.4.a; pure-codim-$1$
indeterminacy for maps into a group variety). The two combine to force the
indeterminacy locus empty; uniqueness is the standard agreement principle
for reduced `X` and separated `A`. -/
theorem extend_to_av
    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
    {X : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    [Smooth X.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
    [IsSeparated X.hom] [LocallyOfFiniteType X.hom]
    [IsIntegral X.left] [IsReduced X.left]
    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom]
    [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
    (f : X.left.RationalMap A.left) :
    ∃! (g : X.left ⟶ A.left), g.toRationalMap = f := by
  sorry

end RationalMap

end Scheme

end AlgebraicGeometry

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
